name: Begonia ferox
origin: Guangxi, China (native to Guangxi Zhuang Autonomous Region, shaded karst limestone)
aboutText: Begonia ferox, described by C.-I.Peng and Yan Liu., is a limestone forest endemic from the Guangxi Zhuang Autonomous Region of southern China — typically found in shaded karst habitats at low to mid elevations, growing on moist, humus-rich calcareous soil and in crevices of limestone rock. The name 'ferox' (fierce) refers to the plant's most extraordinary feature: the upper surface of each thick, heavily bullate leaf is covered with numerous dark conical bullae — hard, spine-like projections rising from the raised surface texture in a pattern that resembles armour or reptile scales. The upper surface is deep green with prominent venation; undersides are maroon to burgundy. Short, densely spiny petioles complete the effect. Ferox has become one of the most sought-after of all collector Begonias since its description in 2015, and remains genuinely difficult to obtain in the UK market.

morphology.venation: Prominent, with deeply sunken areas between bullae
morphology.texture: Thick, heavily bullate with dark conical spine-like projections on upper surface; maroon-burgundy undersides
morphology.growthHabit: Creeping rhizome with compact leaf arrangement

propagation.notes: Most rhizomatous begonias root readily from leaf sections or petiole cuttings placed on moist substrate. Keep warm and humid until plantlets emerge at leaf margins.
